examples.title: Simple IoT Device Authentication
examples.description: Demonstrates token-based authentication for IoT devices using JWT-like tokens
examples.code:
import hashlib
import hmac
import time

class IoTDevice:
    def __init__(self, device_id, secret_key):
        self.device_id = device_id
        self.secret_key = secret_key
        
    def generate_token(self):
        timestamp = str(int(time.time()))
        message = f"{self.device_id}:{timestamp}"
        token = hmac.new(
            self.secret_key.encode(),
            message.encode(),
            hashlib.sha256
        ).hexdigest()
        return f"{timestamp}.{token}"
    
    def authenticate(self, token):
        try:
            timestamp, received_hash = token.split('.')
            # Check timestamp (token valid for 5 minutes)
            if abs(int(timestamp) - int(time.time())) > 300:
                return False
            # Verify token
            message = f"{self.device_id}:{timestamp}"
            expected_hash = hmac.new(
                self.secret_key.encode(),
                message.encode(),
                hashlib.sha256
            ).hexdigest()
            return hmac.compare_digest(expected_hash, received_hash)
        except:
            return False

# Usage
device = IoTDevice("sensor_001", "secret_key_123")
token = device.generate_token()
print(f"Token: {token}")
print(f"Authenticated: {device.authenticate(token)}")
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates lightweight token-based authentication suitable for resource-constrained IoT devices. It uses HMAC-SHA256 for generating and verifying authentication tokens with timestamps to prevent replay attacks.

examples.title: Secure Boot Sequence (Pseudocode)
examples.description: Demonstrates the secure boot verification process using digital signatures
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates secure boot implementation. It verifies cryptographic signatures of firmware before execution, ensuring only authenticated code runs. The chain of trust starts from hardware-secured root keys.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is firmware size

examples.title: IoT Device Provisioning Protocol
examples.description: Secure device enrollment process with certificate-based authentication
examples.code:
ALGORITHM IoTDeviceProvisioning
BEGIN
  // Phase 1: Manufacturing
  GENERATE unique_device_id
  GENERATE asymmetric_key_pair (public_key, private_key)
  STORE private_key in secure_element (TPM)
  EMBED device_id AND public_key in firmware
  
  // Phase 2: Deployment
  DEVICE connects to provisioning server
  SERVER authenticates device using device_id
  SERVER generates X.509 certificate with public_key
  SERVER signs certificate using CA private_key
  SERVER transmits certificate to device securely
  DEVICE stores certificate in secure storage
  
  // Phase 3: Operational
  WHILE device_active DO
    IF renewal_required THEN
      SEND certificate_signing_request TO server
      server_signs_renews_certificate
      device_validates_and_stores_new_cert
    END IF
    IF server_challenge_received THEN
      sign_challenge_using_private_key
      send_signature_to_server
      server_verifies_and_grants_access
    END IF
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows secure device provisioning - the process of enrolling IoT devices into a network with unique identities and cryptographic credentials. It uses X.509 certificates for long-term authentication.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - certificates stored in secure element
